posts.title: FDA’s ‘Peptide Rally’: What Changed — and What Hasn’t (Yet)
posts.subtitle: A plain-English guide to Category 2 removal, the July 2026 PCAC hearing, and why formal rulemaking still matters for 503A compounding
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Peptides and policy concept image used in PeptideKnow coverage of RFK and FDA peptide actions
posts.excerpt: A new analysis from the FDA Law Blog explains why the FDA’s recent ‘Category 2 removal’ headlines don’t automatically make peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500 legal to compound. Here’s what the FDA actually scheduled (PCAC in July 2026), why notice-and-comment rulemaking still matters, and what patients and pharmacies should watch next.
posts.metaDescription: Did the FDA ‘legalize’ peptides like BPC-157 and TB-500? Not yet. Learn what Category 2 removal does (and doesn’t) mean, the July 23–24, 2026 PCAC timeline, and why formal rulemaking still controls 503A compounding access.

posts.title: FDA Sets July 2026 PCAC Hearing for BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, Semax, and More
posts.subtitle: What the FDA will review at the July 23–24 Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee meeting — and what it means for compounded peptides
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: FDA advisory committee meeting concept with peptide vials and regulatory documents
posts.excerpt: The FDA has published the agenda for its July 23–24, 2026 Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) meeting, naming seven peptides—including BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, Semax, and Epitalon—for discussion as potential additions to the 503A bulks list. Here’s what’s on the agenda, what “uses evaluated” means, and what to watch before July.
posts.metaDescription: The FDA’s July 23–24, 2026 PCAC meeting will discuss BPC-157, TB-500, MOTS-C, KPV, DSIP (emideltide), Semax, and Epitalon for possible inclusion on the 503A bulks list. Full breakdown of the agenda, deadlines, and implications for compounded peptides.

posts.title: BRP: Stanford's AI-Discovered Peptide That Could Replace Ozempic
posts.subtitle: A 12-amino-acid molecule found by AI targets only the brain's appetite center — no nausea, no muscle loss, no GI side effects
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: Molecular peptide structure floating above a brain model in a neuroscience laboratory — representing BRP peptide discovery
posts.excerpt: Stanford researchers used an AI algorithm called Peptide Predictor to scan 20,000 human genes and discovered BRP — a naturally occurring 12-amino-acid peptide that reduces food intake by 50% in animal models, causes fat-specific weight loss, and produces none of the nausea, muscle loss, or GI side effects associated with Ozempic. Here's what the Nature paper actually shows, and why it matters.
posts.metaDescription: Stanford scientists used AI to discover BRP (BRINP2-Related Peptide), a naturally occurring 12-amino-acid peptide that suppresses appetite through a novel hypothalamic mechanism — with no nausea, no muscle loss, and no GI side effects seen in animal studies. Full analysis of the Nature paper, mechanism, animal data, and comparison to Ozempic.

posts.title: FDA Peptide Reclassification 2026: Which 12 Peptides Are Being Restored Under RFK Jr.
posts.subtitle: A comprehensive guide to the FDA's reversal on compounded peptides, the PCAC review timeline, and what it means for patients and providers
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: FDA building exterior with laboratory peptide vials representing the 2026 peptide reclassification decision
posts.excerpt: In April 2026, the FDA announced it would remove 12 peptides from its Category 2 list, reversing a sweeping 2023 ban that cut off patient access to compounds like BPC-157, TB-500, and Semax. Here's the full breakdown of what changed, which peptides are affected, and the timeline ahead.
posts.metaDescription: Complete guide to the 2026 FDA peptide reclassification. Learn which 12 peptides (BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, MOTS-C, more) are being restored, the PCAC review timeline, and what RFK Jr.'s MAHA movement means for compounding pharmacies and patient access.
posts.keywords: FDA peptide reclassification 2026, RFK peptides, peptide ban reversal, BPC-157 legal status, TB-500 FDA, compounding pharmacy peptides, PCAC meeting July 2026, Category 2 peptides, MAHA peptides, which peptides are banned

posts.title: How to Submit Public Comments on FDA Peptide Compounding (Docket FDA-2025-N-6895)
posts.subtitle: A step-by-step guide to making your voice heard before the July 2026 PCAC meeting deadline
posts.author: PeptideKnow Editorial
posts.imageAlt: A person typing a public comment letter on a laptop with a government building visible in the background, documents labeled 'Public Comments' and 'Proposed Regulation' on the desk
posts.excerpt: The FDA has opened docket FDA-2025-N-6895 for public comments on seven peptides — including BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, and Epitalon — ahead of the July 23–24, 2026 PCAC hearing. Here's exactly how to submit your comment, request an oral presentation, and make your voice count before the July 9 priority deadline.
posts.metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to submitting public comments to FDA docket FDA-2025-N-6895 before the July 2026 PCAC meeting on peptide compounding. Covers BPC-157, TB-500, Semax, Epitalon, MOTs-C, KPV, and DSIP. Deadlines: July 9 (Committee) and July 22 (docket close).
